topic: Decision Making to Improve Operational Performance
subject: business-studies

items.q: When can a laissez-faire leadership style be effective?
items.correct: When employees are highly skilled, self-motivated and need autonomy — common in creative or expert teams. It can fail with inexperienced staff or in crises requiring direction.
items.misconception: Laissez-faire leadership means the leader doesn't care about the business.
items.random_wrong: Laissez-faire leadership is illegal under employment law.
items.why: Laissez-faire is a deliberate choice to delegate, not negligence. Students moralise it as lazy management, missing its appropriateness for skilled autonomous workers and innovation-led settings.

items.q: How do stakeholder and shareholder interests overlap and conflict?
items.correct: Shareholders are a subset of stakeholders. Their interests overlap (long-term success benefits all) but conflict on issues like dividends vs investment, wages vs profits, or short-term gains vs long-term sustainability.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders always want the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Shareholders are external and stakeholders are internal.
items.why: The shareholder-stakeholder distinction is central to debates on corporate purpose. Students miss the structural tension — a board can't always please everyone simultaneously, which is what stakeholder analysis exists to examine.

items.q: What is the difference between profit and revenue?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity sold); profit is revenue minus total costs — what is left after paying for everything.
items.misconception: Profit and revenue are different words for the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Profit is what businesses earn; revenue is what employees earn.
items.why: A business can have huge revenue but make no profit if costs are equally huge. Conflating them leads to nonsensical analysis — Amazon had high revenue but low profits for years.

items.q: Modigliani and Miller's irrelevance theorem was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1958. “1948” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Cost of capital. “Annual cost calculation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Modigliani and Miller publish their capital structure theorem?
items.why: Exact answer: 1958 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
